[Subagent: researcher]
Reviewed the three candidate sm_120 fp8 runtimes:

| Runtime       | fp8 support | Install burden | Throughput vs vLLM |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| TensorRT-LLM  | native      | high (source)  | 2.3–2.8x            |
| SGLang        | via CUTLASS | moderate       | 1.4–1.7x            |
| vLLM          | reference   | low            | 1.0 (baseline)      |

Recommendation: TensorRT-LLM for production serving once build time is amortized. Keep vLLM as the dev-loop default.

[Subagent: doc-writer]
Drafted a 3-paragraph section for SECURITY.md explaining the refresh-token rotation policy:

> ## Refresh token rotation
>
> Refresh tokens are rotated on every /v1/auth/refresh call. The server
> considers a refresh request valid if `issued_at ≤ now() + SKEW_TOLERANCE`
> where SKEW_TOLERANCE is 30 seconds by default. This tolerance exists
> because auth-service pods can drift from wall-clock by small amounts
> under NTP contention.
>
> If clock skew exceeds SKEW_TOLERANCE, the server emits a structured log
> event `auth.refresh.clock_skew_exceeded` with delta in milliseconds and
> returns 401 STALE_TOKEN.
>
> Rotation uses 256-bit tokens generated with secrets.token_urlsafe(32).
> The previous token is revoked immediately on successful rotation.

[Subagent: debugger]
Reproduced the bug on a local branch. The failing path:

  ToolRegistry.dispatch → PermissionEngine.evaluate → DENY

The permission engine was initialized with `deny_patterns=[]` during the
smoke test. Previously that was accepted; after v0.14 it raises
ValueError because _DENY_REQUIRED floor was added. Two options:
  (a) relax the floor — accept empty deny list, preserve dangerous-shell hard guard
  (b) document the change and update call sites.

Recommendation: (a). The hard guard is sufficient — requiring user-facing deny patterns is arbitrary.

[Subagent: researcher]
Current state of the art for structured LLM output (as of April 2026):

- instructor (Python): mature, Pydantic-first, ~100k stars, most common
- Outlines: grammar-constrained decoding, strong for non-OpenAI models
- Guardrails AI: rule-based validation with retry, more business-logic oriented
- OpenAI structured outputs: native, JSON-schema guaranteed, strict=True
- LiteLLM: passes through whichever of the above your upstream supports

For this project I'd default to instructor over OpenAI's native mode because we target multiple providers.

[Subagent: writer]
Drafted a PROJECT_GUIDE.md section:

## Data generation philosophy

Every synthetic sample must be grounded in real tool execution. This is a
hard rule — not a preference. Models trained on fabricated tool outputs
learn to hallucinate tool-shaped artifacts instead of ground truth.

Stage B (executor) dispatches against the *real* ToolRegistry on a tmp
sandbox. The only exceptions are fixture-backed tools (web_search,
web_fetch, github, pdf_read, image_read, code_search, spawn_agent) which
cannot run deterministically without external services. Their fixtures are
hand-authored and cover ~20 realistic responses each.

[Subagent: debugger]
Traced the crash in auth-service/refresh.py:142.

Root cause: asyncio.gather(return_exceptions=False) propagated a CancelledError upward when the rotation task was cancelled by a client disconnect mid-flight. The enclosing try/except ValueError did not catch CancelledError, so the worker crashed.

Fix: either catch BaseException in the outer scope and translate CancelledError into a normal shutdown, or use asyncio.shield() on the rotation coroutine. Recommend the latter — simpler intent.

[Subagent: researcher]
Documented the differences between ORPO, DPO, and GRPO:

- DPO: preference-pair loss (winner vs loser). No explicit reward model.
- ORPO: adds an odds-ratio regularizer to DPO. Better on smaller preference sets; tolerant of low-quality rejected samples.
- GRPO: group-relative advantage, K samples per prompt. No paired preferences — only a rule-based or model-based reward per completion.

For Bible-AI: ORPO is correct (you have preference pairs from theological reviewers). For tool-calling Godspeed: GRPO is the right choice (reward = tool call correctness, binary).

[Subagent: reviewer]
Compared the two dataset formats:

- Format A: flat list of {messages, tools}. Matches OpenAI's training JSONL.
- Format B: nested {session_id, messages, tools, metadata}. Richer for filtering.

The ml-lab `messages_raw` reader accepts only Format A. Ship A; keep B in an intermediate file if metadata is valuable downstream. Don't try to generalize the reader — it's used by >3 downstream consumers already.
